risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants will be randomly divided into two groups (A and B) of equal size using a computer-generated randomisation schedule with permuted blocks of random size prepared by the study statistician." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated randomisation with permuted blocks, prepared by a statistician), which is sufficient for Low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: Participants will be randomly divided into two groups (A and B) of equal size using a computer-generated randomisation schedule with permuted blocks of random size prepared by the study statistician. The randomisation schedule will not be stratified as the risk of important imbalances in prognostic factors is small for a trial of this size [29].
_evidence.method_summary: Computer-generated randomisation schedule with permuted blocks of random size, prepared by the study statistician.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study medication (nortriptyline or identical placebo) will be packaged in identical containers. Each container will be pre-labelled (by the study pharmacist contracted to provide the study medication) with a study identifier according to randomisation schedule. The contracted pharmacist will determine which group of participants, A or B, will be allocated to receive nortriptyline. Neither the study statistician nor the contracted pharmacist will have any contact with the participants, nor will they be able to influence treatment allocation. Study numbers and study medication will be issued sequentially to participants." Comment: This describes a pharmacist-controlled allocation process using identical containers and sequential issue of study medication, which prevents recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrolment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The study medication (nortriptyline or identical placebo) will be packaged in identical containers. Each container will be pre-labelled (by the study pharmacist contracted to provide the study medication) with a study identifier according to randomisation schedule. The contracted pharmacist will determine which group of participants, A or B, will be allocated to receive nortriptyline. Neither the study statistician nor the contracted pharmacist will have any contact with the participants, nor will they be able to influence treatment allocation. Study numbers and study medication will be issued sequentially to participants.
_evidence.note: Sequence generation is described, and the allocation concealment section states that identical containers were pre-labelled by a pharmacist according to the randomisation schedule and issued sequentially.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study investigators, the research nurse dispensing the medication and assessing outcomes, and the participants will remain blind to the treatment allocation. ... The study medication (nortriptyline or identical placebo) will be packaged in identical containers." Comment: Participants and key study personnel were explicitly blinded, with an identical placebo and identical containers used to support masking. The outcomes include patient-reported measures, so successful blinding is important; the protocol states the blinding was maintained and even assessed for effectiveness.

_evidence.blinding_quote: We propose a parallel group, two-arm, participant and investigator-blinded, randomised controlled superiority trial comparing nortriptyline with placebo. ... The study investigators, the research nurse dispensing the medication and assessing outcomes, and the participants will remain blind to the treatment allocation. ... The study medication (nortriptyline or identical placebo) will be packaged in identical containers.
_evidence.blinding_method: nortriptyline or identical placebo; identical containers; identical placebo capsules
outcome_categories: participant’s belief about treatment arm allocation
subjective_outcomes: participant’s belief about treatment arm allocation

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study investigators, the research nurse dispensing the medication and assessing outcomes, and the participants will remain blind to the treatment allocation." Comment: The outcome assessor (research nurse) was explicitly blinded, and the main outcomes were assessed in a structured interview with participant-reported measures. Because both the assessor and participants were blinded, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The study investigators, the research nurse dispensing the medication and assessing outcomes, and the participants will remain blind to the treatment allocation.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants who are missing outcome data will be included in the analysis using modern multiple imputation methods." Comment: This is a trial protocol and does not report any observed post-randomization attrition numbers for the analysed outcome (no per-arm randomized vs analysed counts, no actual loss-to-follow-up percentage, and no reasons for missing outcome data). Although the analysis plan includes intention-to-treat with multiple imputation and a planned per-protocol analysis, there is no reported attrition on the outcome of interest to show whether missing data could have biased the result; therefore the correct judgement is Unclear risk rather than High or Low.

_evidence.notes: This is a trial protocol; no post-randomisation attrition numbers are reported. The analysis plan states: 'Data analysis will be performed on an intention-to-treat basis' and 'Participants who are missing outcome data will be included in the analysis using modern multiple imputation methods.' A per-protocol analysis was also planned.
